behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Failure is most likely at module boundaries (file discovery -> sort/planning -> execution order), not just inside a pure sort helper. No OS/subprocess/LLM dependency is required to expose it.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp migrations directory with out-of-order filenames (e.g., 010, 002, 001), run the migration runner with a local test DB adapter, and assert execution/history records show apply order 001 -> 002 -> 010.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on persisted state across runs via the _migrations table. Unit tests with mocks can miss real handoff/state bugs between runner logic and metadata storage.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner twice against the same local test database and migration set; assert migration side effects occur once and _migrations contains a single record per version after both runs.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real PostgreSQL transaction/error semantics and failure propagation. Mocked DB behavior can hide rollback and abort-on-failure defects.
behaviors.test_description: Against a real PostgreSQL instance, run migrations where one file performs a write then triggers SQL error, followed by another migration. Assert writes from the failing migration are absent (rollback) and later migration was not applied.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is true concurrent execution across separate processes plus shared DB locking/constraints. In-process mocks or single-process tests can miss race conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two runner processes simultaneously against the same real PostgreSQL database and identical migrations directory; assert each migration’s side effect and _migrations entry appears exactly once total.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: This is bootstrap ordering across runner components and DB metadata initialization. It needs module interaction with a fresh DB state, but not subprocess-level orchestration beyond normal local DB integration.
behaviors.test_description: Start with a fresh empty test database, run the runner once, and assert _migrations exists before first migration bookkeeping/record insert and all migrations then apply successfully.
